sentences.text: Schirach?
Schirach was charged in the conspiracy, count one, as were all the defendants.
And he was also charged on count four, which is crimes against humanity.
And I might tell you how I concluded that case, because I think it's rather dramatic.

In making the presentation-- [CLEARS THROAT] in making and preparing for the presentation, one of the documents we had was a translation of a speech he made in Vienna [CLEARS THROAT] to a number of youth leaders from other countries, semi-fascist youth organizations.

And at the end of it, he said-- and of course, in German-- 'If I am charged with having sent thousands upon thousands of Jews into the ghettos of the East, I consider it a contribution to civilization.' And after I had put in all the other evidence we had, I said, your honors, I'd like to conclude by a statement, which was not only delivered by the defendant von Schirach before youth in Vienna, but which was printed in the word for word, the next day.
And then, I read that.
And then, I mean, I quoted that thing.
And it was-- you could-- and I just said, thank you, and moved away from the podium.
It was a rather striking way to end that presentation.
I think the-- the-- each of the presentations I made did not take longer than an hour and a half.

And I think we'd agreed in advance, and that the tribunal had more or less said-- well, as matter of fact, Commander Albrecht, who presented the case against Goering, got sat on by the court very heavily for making colorful remarks that were not necessary, in order to get the evidence in.
[CLEARS THROAT] And the tribunal said-- President Lawrence said, Commander Albrecht, we don't want to hear any more of that, finally.
Because Albrecht was given to flowery language, colorful language.
And so that was one of the things that gave us some cues, very early, as to what kind of presentation the tribunal wanted.
Now, if you're quoting something very dramatic, such as I did just then, [CLEARS THROAT] just now, if you raised your voice a bit, of course, that was all right.
But they didn't want you making a description of what you were doing, which was too colorful.
They wanted it-- this laid out factually.
[CLEARS THROAT] Yes.

[CLEARS THROAT] In the first place, as the youth leader from way before Hitler came to power-- he was-- he'd been made youth leader even before that, and then became the Reich Youth Leader shortly after Hitler was made chancellor, in January of 1933.

The evidence I presented was mainly statements that he had made at youth gatherings, which were published in the newspapers, [CLEARS THROAT] or pamphlets which he wrote, which I said incited the youth to follow the Nazi conspirators in building a solid Nazi regime, and hence contributed to the making of aggressive war, because-- And then, he did make a number of statements.

One was quoted by a former youth leader, and we used his affidavit, where he talked the fact that-- or about the proposition that Nazi doctrines were so important that they would be accepted, eventually, across the world.
And he put this in a context which indicated that they were already doing some preparations for this.
So I kind of connected him up to, our notion of the conspiracy actually extended toward world domination, eventually.

The tribunal-- very-- President Lawrence said, after I had been going maybe 15 minutes, Captain Sprecher, we understand from what you have said that the defendant, von Schirach, clearly was the main leader of Hitler Youth, and that he made a lot of statements of the kind that you have said, and we think that you've adequately presented that point, subject to the defense responding.
And please go on.
But I still had some material I wanted to get in.
So I tried to bring some more in, and he shut me up again.
So I then said, would your honors be interested in hearing of the relationship between Baldur von Schirach as head of the youth leader and the-- and Himmler, who was the head of the SS?
And this was asking the court, do you want to hear this evidence, which I've got?
Which was kind of a funny question.
And everybody, after a while, said you-- you little bastard.
[LAUGHS]
And he said, well, I don't know.
I haven't seen it.
Actually, the document book had-- in English containing this document was already in front of him, but he hadn't gotten to it.
And this was a-- a document where Schirach had talked very strongly about the Jews, and about the superiority of the Nordic race, and things like this.

And he'd then made an arrangement with Himmler, the head of the SS, that, at a certain stage, Himmler had the right to select some people from the Hitler Youth and put them into special programs, so that they would be-- become members of the SS.
So this was kind of a pulling out-- from the Nazi point of view-- kind of an elite group and pushing them into the SS.
This was very strong evidence.
Oh, yes.
They-- they-- they-- they received the evidence-- that evidence in.
Then I went on to the-- [CLEARS THROAT] the-- to von Schirach, as the gauleiter of Vienna.
Well, a gauleiter is the main government leader of the German Reich-- of the German government-- in a particular area, called a gau, or a province.
And the former country of Austria was divided up into four or five gaus.
And the-- one was the gau Vienna, Gau Wien.

And he was made the gau leader of-- of Vienna, which was quite a prestigious kind of position, because-- and very important, because the Nazis were anxious to take over [CLEARS THROAT] Vienna, not merely in the sense of having occupied it, but in terms of completely weeding out any possible roots of opposition.
A lot of my proof went into the squabble that developed between the Nazi authorities and the churches, particularly of the Catholic Church, in Austria.
Because [CLEARS THROAT] the churches owned a great deal of land.
And the government-- the Nazi government wanted to [CLEARS THROAT] get back a lot of this.
And there were some big, kind of, proceedings, which were loaded, of course, in terms of coming out with the churches losing some of their lands.
So I introduced that evidence, as well.
And Schirach had become quite articulate in damning some of the work of the churches.
So we brought that in as a part of his [CLEARS THROAT] crimes against humanity.
Did you offer any testimony against the defendant?
I beg your pardon?
Any witnesses?
[CLEARS THROAT] No.
We-- I introduced some affidavits.
In that sense, testimony, but no witness.
I didn't call a witness.
And [CLEARS THROAT] basically speaking, there was not much challenge to-- by Schirach, when he took the stand.
If you remember, we were all surprised to see that he came to the point of saying in his final statement-- he didn't say all of this during his-- while he was on the witness stand.
But in his final statement, [CLEARS THROAT] he said, I have been guilty of indoctrinating German youth in-- into the hands of a barbarian, whom I did not recognize as a barbarian at the time.

Now, you know that's-- from the prosecution's point of view and from history's point of view, that's quite a statement to have come from the former [CLEARS THROAT] Hitler Youth leader and gauleiter of Vienna.
I beg your pardon?
The final statements, which is when I think he said it, were not under oath.

That's, incidentally, legally speaking, from the point of view of jurisprudence, an addition that was present in the Nuremberg trial, which does not exist in Anglo-Saxon practice, in American practice, or British practice.
At the end of a trial, a defendant is allowed to speak, just before the tribunal goes into recess to consider its judgement.
And he's not to be questioned by anybody.
He makes any statement he wants.
And this is a part of normal legal practice in the continent of Europe.

Well, he-- his defense basically was that I didn't really think that Hitler was aiming at any kind of war, and that I was greatly impressed by the injustices, which was done-- had been done to Germany at the end of World War I, the Treaty of Versailles, and things like that.

So I gladly followed along, as a young person, and was impressed by what Hitler said, but I never attended any conferences, or any [CLEARS THROAT] secret meetings, nor did I see any secret documents, which indicated the plans for the occupation of Austria, or for [CLEARS THROAT] the invasion of Poland, or any of the other aggressive acts.
And we never could prove that he did see those.
Oh, yes.
He had a lawyer by the name of Dr.
Sauter, S-A-U-T-E-R, who also defended the defendant, Von Ribbentrop, for a time.
But Ribbentrop didn't like him, because Sauter wouldn't perform exactly as Ribbentrop wanted him to.
And so Ribbentrop got another lawyer, and then still another lawyer.
[CLEARS THROAT] But Sauter stayed on as the attorney to-- as the main defense attorney for Schirach.
Well, Sauter was one of the older lawyers who still had left in him some residues of the practice of the law before the Nazis came to power.

And that was also true of [CLEARS THROAT] Rudolph Dix, who was the counsel for Schacht, who undoubtedly, as the-- he had been the president of the German Bar Association before the Nazis came to power.

And within months after the Nazis came to power, he [CLEARS THROAT] signed a document, more or less, turning over the German Bar Association to the German Nazi group, which was led by the defendant, Hans Frank.
Schirach was found [CLEARS THROAT] not guilty on count one, the conspiracy count.

He was found guilty of crimes against humanity, because of his activities in Vienna, as the gau leader of Vienna, and particularly because of he making that statement of having-- which he never denied-- of having made the statement about the thousands and thousands of Jews who had been moved into the ghettos of the East-- 'I consider it a contribution to civilization.'
He had also had a-- a conference with Hitler and somebody else, who I don't remember at the time, of which we had a document.
And in that document, [CLEARS THROAT] Hitler had said, there's still 60,000 Jews in Vienna?
I want you to get rid of them.
And then, that document, we introduced.
Now, it didn't say where they were to be gotten rid of, or how, but they were, of course, pushed into some of the concentration camps, and most of them were killed.
What was the sentence imposed on Schirach for his conviction on crimes against humanity?
20 Years, which he served.
There was two defendants got 20-year sentences, Schirach, and the defendant, Albert Speer.
Both of them served out their sentences.
Schirach wrote a book after a while, but it isn't a very impressive book, about his conversion and that he's-- why he was now against the Nazis, and so forth.
Whereas, Speer wrote a very creditable book, after a while, which contains a pretty accurate statement of how things developed and how he felt.
He'd he was one of the defendants who really had a conversion and studied a lot while he was in Spandau prison during those 20 years.
No.
[DRINKS WATER]
Yes.
No.
No, he died some years back.
I don't know how long back, but he died some years back.
But [CLEARS THROAT] his property, or the property of his family, still remained, so that he was a fairly wealthy man.
He came from a rather distinguished family in-- property-wise, in Germany, anyway.
Had he lived there?
Not that I know of.
The defendant, Schacht, had, of course.
He'd lived in the United States for a time, but not Schirach.
Well, Fritzsche had been a well-known journalist for the conservative newspapers.
And Goebbels, the Reich Propaganda Ministry-- head of the Reich Propaganda Ministry-- thought he was very able.
And so he called him in and said, I want you to become one of the leaders, with respect to the press of Germany.
And Fritzsche accepted and also then became a Nazi.
This was, I think, about April, only four months after Hitler came to-- to power.
Fritzsche was given the task of collecting materials from the various agencies.
And he would pass this to Otto Dietrich, who was the Reich Press Chief.
He and Goebbels were kind of-- although, the press chief, Otto Goebbels, was underneath Goebbels in some way.
I'm sorry, I mispronounced-- I took the wrong word.
Although Dietrich was underneath Goebbels to some extent, he also traveled with Hitler and had a very separate in-road to Hitler.
So Fritzsche was in a position of having to get instructions, you might say, from two top guys and get this converted to language which was satisfactory to both of them, which was quite an art.
And then he would present paroles to the press.
And he did this regularly during the immediate years before the war.
And of course, before each invasion, the press would be colored, somewhat, in terms of what the objects were of the Nazis in invading a country and so forth.
The main thing he did, which I kept repeating in my presentation-- there, I should go back.
The British had-- through the British Broadcasting System-- had intercepted all the main speeches that Fritzsche had made as the head of the Reich radio division of the Propaganda Ministry.
Fritzsche didn't get that position until after the war began.
Then he began making speeches every week, called 'Hans Fritzsche Spricht,' Hans Fritzsche Speaks.
And these were listened to, and a lot of propaganda in them.
These had been intercepted and parts of them translated-- well, they were translated into English.
And then, strangely enough, the British had destroyed the discs, because they needed them for other transcriptions.
So we had the translations, but not the originals.
We put out notices throughout Germany and tried to see if anybody had these.
And nothing came of it.
Later on, in a later trial, a German did turn them in.
He'd kept copies of these things.
But we didn't have them in the first trial.
So I had to introduce [CHUCKLES] German translations for the-- from the English, of-- which was not the original document, which kind of weakened the thing.
But he didn't challenge that too much, because they-- we had so many of them that were so much the same thing, that he decided-- his counsel, whose name was Fritz, Dr.
Fritz, decided that he wouldn't challenge them.
When some of these documents were put through the defense center [CLEARS THROAT] into the hands of the defense counsel, long before the Fritzsche case came up, Dr.
Fritz, his defense counsel, asked to see me.
He came in, and I showed him some of the documents we were going to use.
And he said, I think Fritzsche could explain a lot of this, if you're willing to take the explanations.
And it will improve what you know about the defendant.
And I'm-- we're perfectly willing to tell you this in advance.
And I said, well, prove it to me.
So I would ask questions, through him, of Fritzsche, whom I never interrogated directly.
And then they would bring me back, in German, Fritzsche's response.
I'd have them translated.
And eventually, we made a 20-page affidavit, and which he then signed, just after the Christmas holidays, in 1945.
And that became the principal document that, really, I relied upon in my presentation.
And in there, it was very plain that he said, 'I accuse the international, Bolshevistic, Jewish, capitalist, clique across the world of having caused this war.
And they are the ones who are committing atrocities,' and all this kind of stuff.
So what we tried to do is accuse him-- not that he was telling untruths, but that he was supporting a vicious regime, which was killing people.
We had no proof that he had received documents concerning the Holocaust.
He had been in Russia at the time that the Einsatzgruppen were killing people.
He'd been attached for [CLEARS THROAT] some six months or so, after the Russians-- after the Soviet Union had been invaded, to a propaganda unit of one of the armies.
And no one can believe, in his right mind, that that doesn't mean that he knew what the-- was going on, in the terms of killing a million people.
But we had no proof of it and never could find a witness who could say, we know that Fritzsche knew.
It was a matter of inference.
And the tribunal found him not guilty.
I, of course, disagree with the judgment, but that's the way it was.
He was not found guilty.
No, he was found not guilty.
Yes.
He was found not guilty of being in the conspiracy.
And that, I certainly could agree with, because he was down at about the third level [CLEARS THROAT] in the Propaganda Ministry.
There was Goebbels.
And then, there was a guy by the name of Naumann, who was the assistant.
And then there were eight divisions, of which the radio division was just one.
And that's the division that he was in.
We would never have indicted him in the first trial.
We probably would have indicted him in later trials.
But we would not have indicted him in the first trial, but the Soviets wanted to present somebody they had captured.
And the two defendants they had captured were-- were Raeder, who got a life sentence, and Fritzsche.
And those two defendants were added to make a part of the 24 defendants in the first trial.
Well, yeah, yes.
I should have said that.
He was [CLEARS THROAT] accused of conspiracy, and of crimes against humanity, and I'm quite sure of war crimes too, but I'm not positive of that at the moment.
The proof would have been the same, with respect to whether it was war crimes or crimes against humanity, anyway, so that's not too crucial.
Oh, yes.
No.
Tom Dodd cross-examined-- oh, oh, no.
I beg your pardon.
By that time, [CLEARS THROAT] the-- they're right near the end of the case.

And there was great pressure from the tribunal to-- let's get this over with, because we still have the case on the organizations, the accused organizations, which we're going to have several weeks on that.
[CLEARS THROAT]
So Tom Dodd decided that they had to allow the Soviets to have some cross-examination.
Since they produced him as a defendant, why not let the Soviets cross-examine him?
So they did.
And among other things, Fritzsche denied the so-called Fritzsche confession, which they had obtained from him just after they'd captured him, long before the trial.
And I refused to use it.
I did not-- I declined to use that during my presentation, which was much earlier than the Soviet case, of course.

Partly because of the language in it, and partly because, in dealing with the defense counsel, I was getting these other statements, partly with an understanding that, if I thought Fritzsche was giving me a fair amount of data, even though he was trying to explain it, that I wouldn't use the confession.
So this is a kind of a-- [CLEARS THROAT] an arrangement between the defense counsel and the prosecution counsel, which was approved by Colonel Storey.

In fact, as they were so glad to see some evidence concerning one of the defendants, which wasn't involved in that, quote, Soviet confession, that I-- that's one reason I was then assigned to the Schirach case as well.
And I'm the only assistant trial counsel, actually, that presented two of the cases against the defendants.
[CLEARS THROAT] He wrote a book, called-- I've got it in the other room.
He wrote a book, in which he tried to kind of explain his-- why he'd done things.
He was contrite, to an extent.
[CLEARS THROAT] He talked about his life in jail, and about his experiences, and et cetera.
It isn't a very impressive book.
[CLEARS THROAT]
He does translate it in to English.
And I don't think it sold very-- people weren't too interested in it.
And then, he died very shortly thereafter.
So he never contributed anything further to literature, as did the defendant, Schacht.
And Schacht wrote several books after he was acquitted at Nuremberg.
